text:
COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT
FoundationOne CDx (500-Gene Panel)

---

PATIENT INFORMATION
Patient Name: Marisol Fuentes-Delgado
Date of Birth: [on file]
Sex: Female
Diagnosis: Invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, triple-negative (ER-/PR-/HER2-), left breast
Clinical Stage at Diagnosis: Stage IIB (T2N1M0, AJCC 8th edition)
Ordering Physician: [Oncologist, Breast Medical Oncology]
Institution: [Breast Cancer Program, Academic Medical Center]

SPECIMEN INFORMATION
Specimen ID: [Lab accession number on file]
Specimen Type: Formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded (FFPE) core needle biopsy
Anatomic Site: Left breast, 12 o'clock position (primary tumor)
Procedure: Ultrasound-guided core needle biopsy
Tumor Content (Estimated): 55% (pathologist-reviewed H&E section)
DNA Quality: Adequate for comprehensive genomic profiling
Assay Platform: FoundationOne CDx (FDA-approved companion diagnostic; hybrid capture-based next-generation sequencing of 324 cancer-relevant genes plus select intronic regions; copy number assessment across 500+ genes)
Additional Analyses: Tumor mutational burden (TMB), microsatellite instability (MSI) status, homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) scoring, genomic loss of heterozygosity (gLOH), mutational signature analysis

CLINICAL INDICATION
Comprehensive genomic profiling is requested for a 58-year-old postmenopausal Hispanic woman with newly diagnosed stage IIB triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma, basal-like subtype (CK5/6+, EGFR 2+). Known germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74) identified on germline genetic testing. PD-L1 CPS 28 by 22C3 assay. Ki-67 92%. Tumor sequencing is requested to characterize the somatic mutational landscape, confirm germline findings in the tumor context, assess for additional actionable alterations, evaluate HRD status, and guide treatment planning in the neoadjuvant setting.

---

GENOMIC FINDINGS SUMMARY

This specimen harbors clinically relevant genomic alterations. Findings are categorized below by clinical significance.

---

SECTION 1: CLINICALLY ACTIONABLE ALTERATIONS (Tier I / Tier II)

1. TP53 p.R248W (Missense Substitution)
   - Gene: TP53 (17p13.1)
   - Alteration Type: Somatic point mutation; hotspot missense
   - Variant Allele Frequency (VAF): 68%
   - Predicted Effect: Dominant-negative gain-of-function; loss of wild-type p53 tumor suppressor activity
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic. TP53 p.R248W is one of the most frequent hotspot mutations across human cancers and is among the most recurrent alterations in triple-negative breast cancer. The high VAF (68%) is consistent with heterozygous mutation in a tumor with high cellularity, or possible concurrent loss of the wild-type allele. Strong diffuse p53 immunohistochemical overexpression noted on prior pathology is concordant with this dominant-negative missense mutation. TP53 mutations of this type are associated with aggressive tumor biology, impaired apoptosis, and resistance to certain DNA-damaging agents. No currently approved targeted therapies are directed at TP53 p.R248W specifically; however, this alteration may have implications for prognosis and response to DNA-damaging chemotherapy agents.
   - Tier: IA (well-established pathogenic driver in breast cancer context)

2. BRCA1 Biallelic Inactivation (Germline + Somatic LOH)
   - Gene: BRCA1 (17q21.31)
   - Alteration Type: Germline pathogenic frameshift (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74) confirmed in tumor; somatic loss of heterozygosity (LOH) at the BRCA1 locus detected
   - Germline Variant Classification: Pathogenic (ClinVar, BIC database, ENIGMA consortium classification)
   - Somatic Second Hit: Loss of heterozygosity confirmed, consistent with biallelic inactivation of BRCA1 in the tumor
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; ACTIONABLE. Biallelic BRCA1 inactivation results in complete loss of homologous recombination repair (HRR) capacity in this tumor. This finding is the primary driver of the markedly elevated HRD score (see Section 4) and is directly therapeutically actionable: BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative breast cancer (including TNBC) is an FDA-approved indication for the PARP inhibitor ola (Lynparza; OlympiAD trial) and tala (Talzenna; EMBRACA trial) in the metastatic setting. In the neoadjuvant/adjuvant setting, OlympiA trial data support adjuvant ola for high-risk, HER2-negative, germline BRCA1/2-mutated early breast cancer. Platinum-based chemotherapy agents (Platinol, carbo) also exploit HRR deficiency and demonstrate enhanced activity in BRCA1-mutated TNBC.
   - Companion Diagnostic Status: BRCA1 pathogenic variant; positive for Lynparza eligibility (germline BRCA1/2 mutation in HER2-negative breast cancer) per FDA-approved CDx indication.
   - Tier: IA

3. PTEN Homozygous Deletion
   - Gene: PTEN (10q23.31)
   - Alteration Type: Homozygous deletion (both alleles lost); copy number = 0
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; loss of function. PTEN is a critical negative regulator of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway. Homozygous deletion results in complete loss of PTEN protein expression, leading to constitutive PI3K pathway activation. PTEN loss is present in approximately 20-35% of TNBC and is associated with worse prognosis and potential resistance to certain therapies. PTEN loss may activate the PI3K/AKT pathway in lieu of PIK3CA mutation (see Section 3: PIK3CA wild-type). PTEN-deficient tumors may retain sensitivity to PI3K pathway inhibitors (e.g., alpelisib, though not FDA-approved in TNBC), AKT inhibitors, or mTOR inhibitors in the appropriate context. This finding is consistent with the basal-like TNBC subtype.
   - Tier: IB

4. RB1 p.L153Rfs*11 (Frameshift Deletion)
   - Gene: RB1 (13q14.2)
   - Alteration Type: Somatic frameshift mutation; predicted loss of function
   - VAF: 44%
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; loss of function. RB1 encodes the retinoblastoma protein, a master regulator of the G1/S cell cycle checkpoint. Loss-of-function RB1 mutations are found in approximately 20-25% of TNBC, particularly basal-like subtype. RB1 loss is associated with a high proliferative index (consistent with Ki-67 92% observed in this tumor) and may predict sensitivity to CDK4/6 inhibitors paradoxically in some contexts (though CDK4/6 inhibitors generally require intact RB1 for efficacy in luminal breast cancer; in TNBC with RB1 loss, the biology is distinct). Also associated with increased sensitivity to DNA-damaging agents.
   - Tier: IB

5. CDKN2A p.P114L (Pathogenic Missense)
   - Gene: CDKN2A (9p21.3); also known as p16/INK4A
   - Alteration Type: Somatic pathogenic missense mutation
   - Additional Finding: CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion also detected at the 9p21.3 locus (see Copy Number Alterations section)
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; loss of function. CDKN2A encodes the p16 cell cycle inhibitor, which normally inhibits CDK4/6. Combined somatic point mutation and co-deletion effectively eliminates p16 activity, further dysregulating cell cycle progression. This co-occurs with RB1 loss in a tumor with complete loss of canonical cell cycle control at multiple nodes. CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion is a recurrent finding in aggressive basal-like TNBC.
   - Tier: IB

6. MYC Amplification (High-Level)
   - Gene: MYC (8q24.21)
   - Alteration Type: High-level amplification
   - Copy Number: 18.4 (markedly elevated; normal diploid = 2)
   - Clinical Significance: Pathogenic; gain of function. MYC is a transcription factor and proto-oncogene. High-level MYC amplification is found in approximately 15-20% of TNBC and is associated with aggressive tumor phenotype, high Ki-67, and poor prognosis. MYC amplification may contribute to resistance to certain therapies but also represents a potential synthetic lethality vulnerability (e.g., CDK1 inhibition, BET bromodomain inhibitors targeting MYC transcription). No direct FDA-approved MYC-targeted therapy currently available, but this alteration is clinically significant for prognostication and emerging trial eligibility.
   - Tier: IB

---

SECTION 2: ADDITIONAL SOMATIC ALTERATIONS OF UNCERTAIN OR LIKELY PATHOGENIC SIGNIFICANCE

The following somatic alterations were identified and are listed with their current classification. Variants of uncertain significance (VUS) are reported for informational purposes; clinical decision-making should not be based solely on VUS findings.

1. NF1 p.D1644N
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   - Context: NF1 encodes a RAS-GAP protein; loss-of-function NF1 mutations activate RAS/MAPK signaling. This specific missense variant has limited functional data. NF1 alterations in TNBC may have emerging implications for MEK inhibitor sensitivity (MEK inhibitors such as cobimetinib, tram are under investigation in NF1-altered TNBC).

2. ARID1A p.Q330* (Truncating Nonsense Mutation)
   - Classification: Likely Pathogenic (truncating mutation in a tumor suppressor gene)
   - Context: ARID1A encodes a chromatin remodeling protein (SWI/SNF complex). Truncating ARID1A mutations are frequent in multiple cancer types and are generally considered loss-of-function. ARID1A loss may have implications for synthetic lethality approaches (e.g., EZH2 inhibitors, ARID1A-EZH2 synthetic lethality; ATR inhibitors). This finding may become clinically actionable in the investigational setting.

3. FAT3 p.S2152L
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   - Context: FAT3 is a cadherin family member; limited clinical data in breast cancer.

4. SPEN p.R2196H
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   - Context: SPEN encodes a transcriptional repressor; limited clinical significance in TNBC at this time.

5. RNF43 p.G659Vfs*41 (Frameshift)
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS; truncating alteration)
   - Context: RNF43 is a negative regulator of Wnt signaling. Truncating RNF43 mutations can activate Wnt pathway; porcupine inhibitors (LGK-974, WNT-974) are under clinical investigation in RNF43-altered tumors. This finding is noted for potential future trial eligibility.

6. KDM6A p.R479C
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   - Context: KDM6A (UTX) encodes a histone H3K27 demethylase and chromatin modifier. Loss-of-function KDM6A alterations have been described in various cancers; clinical significance in TNBC is under investigation.

7. ZFHX3 p.D1636N
   - Classification: Variant of Uncertain Significance (VUS)
   - Context: ZFHX3 is a zinc finger homeodomain transcription factor; limited clinical significance at this time.

Notable Absence:
- PIK3CA: Wild-type. No PIK3CA hotspot mutations detected (exons 9 or 20 negative). This is consistent with the TNBC/basal-like phenotype (PIK3CA mutations are far more common in HR-positive breast cancers). Pathway activation in this tumor appears to be driven by PTEN homozygous deletion rather than PIK3CA mutation.
- ESR1: Wild-type. No estrogen receptor ligand-binding domain mutations. Not clinically relevant given ER-negative status.
- AKT1: Wild-type in primary tumor (no E17K or other activating AKT1 mutations detected in this primary specimen).
- KRAS, NRAS, HRAS: Wild-type.
- BRAF: Wild-type.

---

SECTION 3: GENE FUSIONS AND REARRANGEMENTS

Fusion/Rearrangement Analysis: No clinically significant gene fusions detected.
- NTRK1/2/3 fusions: Not detected. Patient is not a candidate for larotrectinib or entrectinib based on this result.
- ALK, ROS1, RET fusions: Not detected.
- FGFR1/2/3 fusions: Not detected.
- ERBB2 rearrangements: Not detected.

---

SECTION 4: COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS

The following copy number alterations (CNAs) were detected by genome-wide copy number analysis:

| Alteration                    | Chromosome | Copy Number / Status       | Significance                          |
|-------------------------------|------------|----------------------------|---------------------------------------|
| MYC amplification             | 8q24       | 18.4 (high-level amp)      | Oncogene amplification; aggressive    |
| PTEN homozygous deletion      | 10q23      | 0 (both alleles lost)      | TSG loss; PI3K pathway activation     |
| CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion     | 9p21       | Homozygous deletion        | TSG loss; cell cycle dysregulation    |
| RB1 deletion                  | 13q14      | Hemizygous deletion        | TSG loss (concurrent with RB1 mut)    |
| MDM4 amplification            | 1q32       | Amplified                  | TP53 pathway; p53 functional loss     |

Note on MDM4 amplification: MDM4 (MDMX) is a negative regulator of p53. MDM4 amplification combined with TP53 p.R248W provides dual mechanisms of p53 pathway disruption in this tumor. MDM4 amplification at 1q32 is also commonly co-occurring with other 1q gains in aggressive TNBC.

Background genomic instability: Genome-wide CNA analysis reveals high chromosomal instability, consistent with BRCA1-deficient TNBC. Multiple regions of allelic imbalance and LOH detected throughout the genome (see gLOH, Section 6).

---

SECTION 5: MICROSATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) AND TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB)

Microsatellite Status: MICROSATELLITE STABLE (MSS)
- Assessment based on analysis of 95 intronic homopolymer and dinucleotide repeat loci.
- No evidence of mismatch repair deficiency (dMMR) by genomic profiling.
- Clinical implication: Keytruda (Keytruda) and other anti-PD-1/PD-L1 agents are NOT indicated solely on the basis of MSI/dMMR in this patient (tumor is MSS). However, PD-L1 CPS of 28 (by 22C3 immunohistochemistry) independently supports pembro use in the TNBC context per KEYNOTE-522 (neoadjuvant) and KEYNOTE-355 (metastatic) trial data and current FDA approvals.

Tumor Mutational Burden (TMB): 9 mutations per megabase (mut/Mb)
- Classification: Intermediate TMB (TMB-Low defined as <6 mut/Mb; TMB-High defined as >=10 mut/Mb per FDA threshold)
- Clinical Implication: This value does not meet the FDA TMB-High threshold (10 mut/Mb) that supports Keytruda use based on TMB alone (per Keytruda pan-tumor TMB-H indication). TMB at 9 mut/Mb is near the threshold; however, current evidence does not support clinical decision-making based on borderline TMB values in breast cancer specifically. The intermediate TMB in this BRCA1-deficient tumor is notable, as BRCA1-mutated TNBC can have variable TMB, and the HRD-associated mutational patterns (see Section 7) are more clinically relevant than TMB in this context.

---

SECTION 6: HOMOLOGOUS RECOMBINATION DEFICIENCY (HRD)

HRD Score: 62 (HIGH; above validated threshold of 42)
Genomic LOH (gLOH): 24% genome-wide loss of heterozygosity
Mutational Signature: SBS3 dominant (see Section 7)

HRD Methodology: The HRD score is a composite genomic scar metric derived from three components:
1. Loss of Heterozygosity (LOH) index
2. Telomeric Allelic Imbalance (TAI) index
3. Large-scale State Transitions (LST) index

Each component reflects the cumulative genomic scarring caused by prior deficiency in homologous recombination repair. An HRD score >=42 is considered HRD-positive by validated thresholds (Myriad myChoice CDx methodology; FoundationOne CDx uses concordant methodology).

Clinical Significance: HRD score of 62, combined with confirmed biallelic BRCA1 inactivation and dominant SBS3 mutational signature, establishes this tumor as definitively homologous recombination-deficient. This has the following clinical implications:
- PARP Inhibitor Sensitivity: Strong predictor of response to PARP inhibitors (ola, Talzenna, Zejula, ruca). Lynparza (Lynparza) is FDA-approved for germline BRCA1/2-mutated HER2-negative advanced breast cancer.
- Platinum Sensitivity: HRD-high status, particularly driven by BRCA1 loss, predicts enhanced sensitivity to platinum-based chemotherapy (cis, Paraplatin). This is particularly relevant in the neoadjuvant setting (e.g., BRCA-BRCAness subgroup of GeparSixto, BrighTNess trial).
- Neoadjuvant Immunotherapy Context: KEYNOTE-522 supports Keytruda addition to neoadjuvant chemotherapy in early TNBC regardless of HRD status, but the HRD-high profile supports anticipated tumor immunogenicity and treatment response.

gLOH of 24%: Consistent with marked genomic instability secondary to BRCA1-mediated HRR deficiency. High gLOH is an independent predictor of platinum and PARP inhibitor benefit.

---

SECTION 7: MUTATIONAL SIGNATURE ANALYSIS

Dominant Signature: SBS3 (COSMIC v3.3)
- SBS3 is the canonical signature associated with defective homologous recombination repair (BRCA1/2 dysfunction). It is characterized by an elevated burden of small insertions and deletions at microhomology junctions and a broad pattern of base substitutions. The dominance of SBS3 in this tumor is highly consistent with the confirmed BRCA1 biallelic inactivation.

Secondary Signatures:
- SBS5 (clock-like, aging-associated): Minor contribution. Expected background signature in a 58-year-old patient.
- SBS13 (APOBEC-associated; C>G at TCN context): Minor contribution detected. APOBEC activity is commonly upregulated in breast cancers with TP53 mutations and genomic instability.

Signature Interpretation: The mutational signature profile of this tumor is dominated by HRD-associated mutagenesis (SBS3), strongly consistent with the underlying BRCA1 germline and somatic inactivation. No tobacco-associated signatures (SBS4) identified, consistent with remote prior smoking history and absence of APOBEC/tobacco-driven biology as a major driver. No signatures associated with prior therapeutic exposures identified (no SBS17 at this time point).

---

SECTION 8: ADDITIONAL BIOMARKERS

HER2 Amplification: Not detected (consistent with IHC HER2 1+ and non-amplified FISH result). No ERBB2 copy number gain. No ERBB2 activating mutations (e.g., exon 20 insertions, kinase domain mutations).

HER3 (ERBB3): No activating mutations detected.

FGFR1/2/3/4: No amplification or activating mutations detected.

AR (Androgen Receptor): AR pathway not assessed by this assay; prior IHC negative (<1%).

PIK3CA: Wild-type (confirmed; no hotspot mutations at E542K, E545K, H1047R/L, or other recurrent sites). Note: Alpelisib (Piqray), which is FDA-approved for PIK3CA-mutated HR-positive/HER2-negative breast cancer, is NOT applicable in this patient (both ER-negative AND PIK3CA wild-type).

CDH1: No pathogenic CDH1 mutations detected (consistent with invasive ductal rather than lobular histology; E-cadherin intact on IHC).

PALB2, RAD51C, RAD51D: No additional germline or somatic pathogenic variants in these HRR-related genes beyond the known BRCA1 mutation. No RAD51C/D mutations detected as additional somatic events in this primary tumor.

---

SECTION 9: GERMLINE FINDINGS NOTE

This tumor-only comprehensive genomic profiling assay confirms the previously identified germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant (c.5266dupC, p.Gln1756Profs*74) in the tumor specimen context, with somatic LOH confirming biallelic inactivation. This report is not a substitute for germline genetic testing or formal genetic counseling. Germline BRCA1/2 testing has been previously performed and genetic counseling has been completed per clinical documentation. First-degree relatives of the patient should be offered genetic counseling and cascade testing given the confirmed pathogenic BRCA1 germline variant.

---

SUMMARY OF CLINICALLY RELEVANT FINDINGS

| Finding                              | Category              | Clinical Relevance                                                      |
|--------------------------------------|-----------------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| BRCA1 biallelic inactivation         | Actionable - Tier IA  | PARP inhibitor eligibility; platinum sensitivity; HRD-high              |
| TP53 p.R248W (VAF 68%)               | Actionable - Tier IA  | Aggressive biology; dominant-negative p53; prognostic                  |
| PTEN homozygous deletion             | Actionable - Tier IB  | PI3K pathway activation; potential AKT/mTOR inhibitor relevance         |
| MYC amplification (CN 18.4)          | Actionable - Tier IB  | Aggressive phenotype; BET inhibitor vulnerability (investigational)     |
| RB1 p.L153Rfs*11 + RB1 deletion     | Actionable - Tier IB  | Cell cycle dysregulation; aggressive biology                            |
| CDKN2A/CDKN2B co-deletion + mutation | Actionable - Tier IB  | Cell cycle dysregulation; aggressive biology                            |
| MDM4 amplification                   | Tier IB               | Dual p53 pathway disruption                                             |
| HRD Score: 62 (HIGH)                 | Biomarker             | Confirms HRR deficiency; PARP inhibitor and platinum sensitivity        |
| MSS; TMB 9 mut/Mb                    | Biomarker             | Not TMB-High; MSS; PD-L1 CPS 28 supports Keytruda (IHC-based)    |
| SBS3 dominant signature              | Biomarker             | Confirms HRD; consistent with BRCA1 inactivation                       |
| No PIK3CA mutations                  | Negative finding      | Alpelisib not applicable                                                |
| No NTRK fusions                      | Negative finding      | TRK inhibitors not applicable                                           |
| No AKT1 mutations (primary)          | Negative finding      | AKT inhibitors not indicated at this time                               |

---

ASSAY PERFORMANCE AND QUALITY METRICS

- Mean exon coverage depth: 624x
- Percentage of targeted bases with >=100x coverage: 99.1%
- Tumor purity (pathologist-estimated): 55%
- Minimum detectable VAF (at this purity): ~5%
- Total reportable somatic alterations (short variants): 14
- Copy number alterations detected: 5 recurrent regions
- All quality control metrics within acceptable parameters for clinical reporting.

---

REPORTING PATHOLOGIST / MOLECULAR ONCOLOGY ATTESTATION

This report was reviewed and authorized by the Laboratory Medical Director, Molecular Pathology and Genomics.

Test performed: FoundationOne CDx (Foundation Medicine, Inc.; FDA-approved PMA P170019)
Laboratory: Foundation Medicine, Inc., Cambridge, MA (or institutional equivalent CLIA-certified laboratory)
CAP/CLIA Accredited: Yes

Note: This report reflects findings from the tested specimen only. Genomic heterogeneity may result in additional alterations not captured from this sampling site. Results should be interpreted in the full clinical, pathological, and molecular context by the treating oncologist.

---

END OF REPORT
